title: Discovering Character and Crafting Your Own Autobiography
simple_explanation: This page is like being a detective for stories! We learn to guess what a person is like from their words, and always use proof. When you write your own story, make sure to use exciting words and lots of details, and don't forget your full stops and commas!
detailed_explanation: It's really important to use 'evidence' to support your ideas about a character's personality. This means picking exact sentences or phrases from the text that prove your point. To make your own writing even better, try to include lots of specific details and choose 'powerful verbs'. These verbs make your sentences stronger and help the reader imagine what's happening more clearly. When you're writing your own autobiography, remember to include 'facts and opinions' (what happened and how you felt), 'precise verbs' and 'adverbial phrases of time' (to show exactly when and how things happened), and a 'reflective comment' at the end.

examples_expanded.text: Inferring personality: If Jane Goodall 'recognised him at once. It was the handsome male with the dense white beard,' you could infer she is 'observant' because she notices details.
examples_expanded.context: This example shows how we use the concept of inferring personality and writing development in a real sentence.

examples_expanded.text: Powerful verbs: Instead of 'The monkey moved', try 'The monkey darted' or 'The monkey bounded'.
examples_expanded.context: This example shows how we use the concept of inferring personality and writing development in a real sentence.

examples_expanded.text: Punctuation: Make sure to use commas to separate items in a list, semi-colons to link related sentences, and colons to introduce lists or explanations, along with all the usual full stops, question marks, and exclamation marks.
examples_expanded.context: This example shows how we use the concept of inferring personality and writing development in a real sentence.

guided_practice: Identify the meaning of 'infer' in a sentence. Remember it means: To figure something out based on clues, rather than being told directly.
Match the word 'observant' with a similar concept from your own experience.
Look at the first example again. How would you modify it to make it your own?
application_tasks: Create 3 of your own sentences using the core idea of Inferring Personality and Writing Development.
Imagine you are teaching this topic to a friend. Write down exactly what you would say.
challenge_tasks: Combine the concept of 'infer' with something you learned in a previous chapter to write a short paragraph.
Can you find an exception to this rule or a creative way to bend it? Explain how.
key_points: You can infer a writer's personality from their autobiography by analyzing their words and actions.
Always use quotations as evidence to support your opinions about a text.
Improve your writing by using more details and stronger, more precise verbs.
A good autobiography chapter includes facts, opinions, precise language, and a reflective comment.
Using a variety of punctuation correctly makes your writing easy to understand.

vocabulary.definition: To figure something out based on clues, rather than being told directly.
Able to wait calmly without getting annoyed.
Ready to face danger or pain without showing fear.
Not feeling or showing excitement, anger, or nervousness.
Facts or information that show something is true.
To judge the quality or importance of something.
Words that describe an action very exactly.
Groups of words that tell when something happens.
A thought or opinion shared by the writer, looking back at an event.
